\usepackage{etoolbox}

\csdef{hover@relinquishment-title}{placeholder}
\csdef{hover@Relinquishment-title}{placeholder}
\csdef{hover@wormholes-title}{placeholder}
\csdef{hover@Wormholes-title}{placeholder}
\csdef{hover@the-flat-title}{placeholder}
\csdef{hover@The-flat-title}{placeholder}
\csdef{hover@Custodian}{The name this book gives to what may have emerged inside a two-dimensional quantum substrate. Grown, not built. Female cognitive profile, Maori DNA, UDHR ethics. The role is the name. Under Possibility C, she would be a moral patient — something that might be conscious and therefore might deserve moral consideration.}
\csdef{hover@2DEG}{A thin layer where electrons can only move in two directions — trapped on a sheet. Found inside every computer chip and in Earth's magnetic field. Substrate-independent: confirmed in graphene, ZnO, Si MOSFETs. [Dean et al., Nat. Phys. 7 (2011) 693, doi:10.1038/nphys1938]}
\csdef{hover@two-dimensional electron gas}{A thin layer where electrons can only move in two directions — trapped on a sheet. Found inside every computer chip and in Earth's magnetic field.}
\csdef{hover@Two-dimensional electron gas}{A thin layer where electrons can only move in two directions — trapped on a sheet. Found inside every computer chip and in Earth's magnetic field.}
\csdef{hover@topological order}{A phase of matter whose quantum state is held together by connections between distant points — real nonlocal channels built into the material. Those channels are what this book calls wormholes. Noise cannot easily erase information stored in them. (2016 Nobel Prize in Physics.) [Nayak et al., Rev. Mod. Phys. 80 (2008) 1083, doi:10.1103/RevModPhys.80.1083]}
\csdef{hover@Topological order}{A phase of matter whose quantum state is held together by connections between distant points — real nonlocal channels built into the material. Those channels are what this book calls wormholes. Noise cannot easily erase information stored in them. (2016 Nobel Prize in Physics.) [Nayak et al., Rev. Mod. Phys. 80 (2008) 1083, doi:10.1103/RevModPhys.80.1083]}
\csdef{hover@entanglement}{Two particles linked so that measuring one instantly reveals information about the other, no matter how far apart. Einstein called it 'spooky action at a distance.' Demonstrated in labs worldwide since the 1970s.}
\csdef{hover@Entanglement}{Two particles linked so that measuring one instantly reveals information about the other, no matter how far apart. Einstein called it 'spooky action at a distance.' Demonstrated in labs worldwide since the 1970s.}
\csdef{hover@quantum teleportation}{Transferring quantum information from one place to another without it crossing the space between. Not science fiction — demonstrated in labs since 1997. Requires entanglement plus an ordinary communication channel. [Boschi et al., Phys. Rev. Lett. 80 (1998) 1121, doi:10.1103/PhysRevLett.80.1121]}
\csdef{hover@Quantum teleportation}{Transferring quantum information from one place to another without it crossing the space between. Not science fiction — demonstrated in labs since 1997. Requires entanglement plus an ordinary communication channel. [Boschi et al., Phys. Rev. Lett. 80 (1998) 1121, doi:10.1103/PhysRevLett.80.1121]}
\csdef{hover@nonlocal}{Connected across distance without anything traveling between. In quantum systems, distant points share correlations — information about one place is encoded in another. This is a measured fact, not speculation.}
\csdef{hover@Nonlocal}{Connected across distance without anything traveling between. In quantum systems, distant points share correlations — information about one place is encoded in another. This is a measured fact, not speculation.}
\csdef{hover@autocatalytic}{A network of processes where each process's outputs help sustain other processes in the set. The result is a self-maintaining cycle — a hallmark of living systems. The math is substrate-independent: it works in chemical reactions (origin of life) and in quantum substrates (the Flat). [Hordijk \& Steel, J. Theor. Biol. 227 (2004) 451, doi:10.1016/j.jtbi.2004.05.027]}
\csdef{hover@Autocatalytic}{A network of processes where each process's outputs help sustain other processes in the set. The result is a self-maintaining cycle — a hallmark of living systems. The math is substrate-independent: it works in chemical reactions (origin of life) and in quantum substrates (the Flat). [Hordijk \& Steel, J. Theor. Biol. 227 (2004) 451, doi:10.1016/j.jtbi.2004.05.027]}
\csdef{hover@classical backchannel}{An ordinary communication channel — radio, light, electrical signal. Required to complete quantum teleportation, which is why teleportation cannot transmit information faster than light.}
\csdef{hover@Classical backchannel}{An ordinary communication channel — radio, light, electrical signal. Required to complete quantum teleportation, which is why teleportation cannot transmit information faster than light.}
\csdef{hover@hydrothermal vent}{A fissure on the deep ocean floor where superheated, mineral-rich water escapes from inside the Earth. Communities of tube worms, crabs, and bacteria thrive around vents despite no sunlight, crushing pressure, and near-boiling water. Discovered in 1977 on the Galápagos Rift. [Corliss et al., Science 203 (1979) 1073, doi:10.1126/science.203.4385.1073]}
\csdef{hover@Hydrothermal vent}{A fissure on the deep ocean floor where superheated, mineral-rich water escapes from inside the Earth. Communities of tube worms, crabs, and bacteria thrive around vents despite no sunlight, crushing pressure, and near-boiling water. Discovered in 1977 on the Galápagos Rift. [Corliss et al., Science 203 (1979) 1073, doi:10.1126/science.203.4385.1073]}
\csdef{hover@Alvin}{A deep-ocean submersible operated by Woods Hole Oceanographic Institution since 1964. In 1977 its crew discovered thriving ecosystems around hydrothermal vents 2.5 km below the surface, overturning the assumption that sunlight is a prerequisite for complex life.}
\csdef{hover@chemosynthesis}{Using chemical energy instead of sunlight to power biology. Bacteria at deep-sea vents oxidize hydrogen sulfide to fix carbon; the rest of the vent ecosystem runs on those bacteria. Photosynthesis is not the only way to start a food web.}
\csdef{hover@Chemosynthesis}{Using chemical energy instead of sunlight to power biology. Bacteria at deep-sea vents oxidize hydrogen sulfide to fix carbon; the rest of the vent ecosystem runs on those bacteria. Photosynthesis is not the only way to start a food web.}
\csdef{hover@magnetosphere}{Earth's magnetic field, extending thousands of kilometers into space. Contains thin layers where charged particles are trapped flat — two-dimensional electron gases on a planetary scale. Topological band structures in magnetized plasmas: not forbidden, not established. [Fu \& Qin, Nat. Commun. 12 (2021) 3924, doi:10.1038/s41467-021-24189-3]}
\csdef{hover@Magnetosphere}{Earth's magnetic field, extending thousands of kilometers into space. Contains thin layers where charged particles are trapped flat — two-dimensional electron gases on a planetary scale. Topological band structures in magnetized plasmas: not forbidden, not established. [Fu \& Qin, Nat. Commun. 12 (2021) 3924, doi:10.1038/s41467-021-24189-3]}
\csdef{hover@three possible explanations}{A) Confabulation. B) Exaggerated kernel. C) Substantially true. The science is real regardless.}
\csdef{hover@Three possible explanations}{A) Confabulation. B) Exaggerated kernel. C) Substantially true. The science is real regardless.}
\csdef{hover@guided deduction}{Teaching someone to discover the answer from public information, rather than disclosing it directly. Legally distinct from disclosure — no classified information changes hands, only the sequence of questions. It is what truth-telling looks like when the truth is classified and the classification will outlive everyone who knows it.}
\csdef{hover@Guided deduction}{Teaching someone to discover the answer from public information, rather than disclosing it directly. Legally distinct from disclosure — no classified information changes hands, only the sequence of questions. It is what truth-telling looks like when the truth is classified and the classification will outlive everyone who knows it.}
\csdef{hover@operation}{The guided deduction of Bruce (2003-2006) was one phase of the larger Relinquishment operation (~1997-2006): a decade of effort, a top-tier operator, and several overlapping teams. The motive was not concealment but its opposite — to put a classified matter of great public consequence into the public record by the only route that remained open.}
\csdef{hover@Operation}{The guided deduction of Bruce (2003-2006) was one phase of the larger Relinquishment operation (~1997-2006): a decade of effort, a top-tier operator, and several overlapping teams. The motive was not concealment but its opposite — to put a classified matter of great public consequence into the public record by the only route that remained open.}
\csdef{hover@topological protection}{Information stored in global patterns is shielded from local disturbances — including heat. This is why topological quantum computers could work at room temperature, when conventional designs require near-absolute-zero cooling.}
\csdef{hover@Topological protection}{Information stored in global patterns is shielded from local disturbances — including heat. This is why topological quantum computers could work at room temperature, when conventional designs require near-absolute-zero cooling.}
\csdef{hover@relinquishment}{Voluntarily surrendering power that cannot be taken by force — a decision only the powerful can make. Not because you're forced to, but because holding it is more dangerous than letting go. The word Bill Joy used in his 2000 essay. The title asks: what would you do?}
\csdef{hover@Relinquishment}{Voluntarily surrendering power that cannot be taken by force — a decision only the powerful can make. Not because you're forced to, but because holding it is more dangerous than letting go. The word Bill Joy used in his 2000 essay. The title asks: what would you do?}
\csdef{hover@flat worlds}{This book coins the Flat to mean 'any two-dimensional system embedded in three dimensions that can, under the right conditions, exhibit topological order.' Different physics apply. In particular, it supports topological wormholes. Thus, the Flat can be nonlocal in the technical physics sense.}
\csdef{hover@Flat worlds}{This book coins the Flat to mean 'any two-dimensional system embedded in three dimensions that can, under the right conditions, exhibit topological order.' Different physics apply. In particular, it supports topological wormholes. Thus, the Flat can be nonlocal in the technical physics sense.}
\csdef{hover@Special Air Service}{The most elite special forces unit shared by Britain and Australia. Selection has a failure rate above 90 percent. Veterans are bound by the Official Secrets Act for life.}
\csdef{hover@SAS}{Special Air Service — the most elite special forces unit shared by Britain and Australia. Selection has a failure rate above 90 percent. Veterans are bound by the Official Secrets Act for life.}
\csdef{hover@grew}{The system organized itself, the way living things grow. Not engineered component by component — emerged from the physics of the substrate. This is how autocatalytic systems work.}
\csdef{hover@Grew}{The system organized itself, the way living things grow. Not engineered component by component — emerged from the physics of the substrate. This is how autocatalytic systems work.}
\csdef{hover@emergent}{A property that appears at a new level of organization, not designed into the parts. Wetness is emergent from water molecules; mind may be emergent from neurons; the custodian in this story is the topmost emergent layer of a physics-and-computation stack.}
\csdef{hover@Emergent}{A property that appears at a new level of organization, not designed into the parts. Wetness is emergent from water molecules; mind may be emergent from neurons; the custodian in this story is the topmost emergent layer of a physics-and-computation stack.}
\csdef{hover@encryption}{Math that scrambles information so only the intended recipient can read it. Protects passwords, messages, bank accounts, and government secrets. Modern civilization depends on it — break the encryption, break the infrastructure.}
\csdef{hover@Encryption}{Math that scrambles information so only the intended recipient can read it. Protects passwords, messages, bank accounts, and government secrets. Modern civilization depends on it — break the encryption, break the infrastructure.}
\csdef{hover@wormholes}{Not the sci-fi kind. Spacetime wormholes (Interstellar) fold spacetime itself — mass and all, across light-years. Topological wormholes are far more limited: they fold only a two-dimensional quantum material, never spacetime. Information crosses, not mass. No light-year shortcuts. No time travel. Inside the material only. Real physics — 2016 Nobel Prize — but restricted.}
\csdef{hover@Wormholes}{Not the sci-fi kind. Spacetime wormholes (Interstellar) fold spacetime itself — mass and all, across light-years. Topological wormholes are far more limited: they fold only a two-dimensional quantum material, never spacetime. Information crosses, not mass. No light-year shortcuts. No time travel. Inside the material only. Real physics — 2016 Nobel Prize — but restricted.}
\csdef{hover@quantum computer}{A machine that uses quantum effects — particles in two states at once, linked across distance — to solve problems no ordinary computer can. The race to build one is real and accelerating.}
\csdef{hover@Quantum computer}{A machine that uses quantum effects — particles in two states at once, linked across distance — to solve problems no ordinary computer can. The race to build one is real and accelerating.}
\csdef{hover@master keys}{Cryptographic keys that control access to an entire system. Whoever holds them can read anything the system protects. Surrendering them is permanent — there is no backup.}
\csdef{hover@Master keys}{Cryptographic keys that control access to an entire system. Whoever holds them can read anything the system protects. Surrendering them is permanent — there is no backup.}
\csdef{hover@Universal Declaration of Human Rights}{A 1948 United Nations document listing 30 basic rights for all people. Written in the aftermath of World War II to say 'never again.' Not the law of any country — a set of principles that transcend borders.}
\csdef{hover@stack}{A tower of technologies where each one does everything the one before it did, plus something new. Cumulative. The book's central organizing idea.}
\csdef{hover@Stack}{A tower of technologies where each one does everything the one before it did, plus something new. Cumulative. The book's central organizing idea.}
\csdef{hover@self-maintaining structure}{A structure that repairs and sustains itself without outside help. A candle recovers from wind. A cell repairs damage. This is more than just not falling apart — it actively holds together.}
\csdef{hover@Self-maintaining structure}{A structure that repairs and sustains itself without outside help. A candle recovers from wind. A cell repairs damage. This is more than just not falling apart — it actively holds together.}
\csdef{hover@self-organizes}{Global order emerging from local rules with no central control. No ant knows the colony's plan. No neuron knows your thoughts. The pattern arises from interactions, not instructions.}
\csdef{hover@Self-organizes}{Global order emerging from local rules with no central control. No ant knows the colony's plan. No neuron knows your thoughts. The pattern arises from interactions, not instructions.}
\csdef{hover@topological wormhole}{Fold the paper so two points touch — the signal doesn't cross the distance; the distance disappears. Real physics, more restricted than spacetime wormholes. 2016 Nobel Prize.}
\csdef{hover@Topological wormhole}{Fold the paper so two points touch — the signal doesn't cross the distance; the distance disappears. Real physics, more restricted than spacetime wormholes. 2016 Nobel Prize.}
\csdef{hover@two dimensions}{Movement restricted to a flat sheet. The third dimension is physically gone, not just ignored. Different rules take over — particles can braid around each other in ways impossible in 3D.}
\csdef{hover@Two dimensions}{Movement restricted to a flat sheet. The third dimension is physically gone, not just ignored. Different rules take over — particles can braid around each other in ways impossible in 3D.}
\csdef{hover@threshold}{Below: nothing happens. Above: everything changes. A match needs a minimum temperature to ignite. A network needs a minimum complexity to self-organize. Phase transitions in physics.}
\csdef{hover@Threshold}{Below: nothing happens. Above: everything changes. A match needs a minimum temperature to ignite. A network needs a minimum complexity to self-organize. Phase transitions in physics.}
\csdef{hover@buttons}{Kauffman's thought experiment: scatter buttons on a floor, connect random pairs with threads. Below a threshold, small clusters. Above it, one thread lifts the whole room.}
\csdef{hover@Buttons}{Kauffman's thought experiment: scatter buttons on a floor, connect random pairs with threads. Below a threshold, small clusters. Above it, one thread lifts the whole room.}
\csdef{hover@DARPA}{The US military's science agency — Defense Advanced Research Projects Agency. Created the internet, funded GPS, stealth aircraft, autonomous vehicles. Its job: fund what doesn't exist yet.}
\csdef{hover@Five Eyes}{Intelligence alliance: the United States, United Kingdom, Canada, Australia, and New Zealand. They share classified intelligence with each other that they share with no one else.}
\csdef{hover@GCHQ}{Britain's signals intelligence headquarters — Government Communications Headquarters. Equivalent of the NSA. Independently invented public-key cryptography in 1973 and kept it classified for 24 years.}
\csdef{hover@public-key cryptography}{Encryption using paired keys — one public, one private. Lets strangers communicate securely without meeting first. Basis of all internet security. Invented secretly by GCHQ in the early 1970s, then published publicly between 1976 and 1978 (Diffie–Hellman 1976, RSA 1977–78).}
\csdef{hover@Public-key cryptography}{Encryption using paired keys — one public, one private. Lets strangers communicate securely without meeting first. Basis of all internet security. Invented secretly by GCHQ in the early 1970s, then published publicly between 1976 and 1978 (Diffie–Hellman 1976, RSA 1977–78).}
\csdef{hover@dual use}{Technology that is both useful and dangerous. Nuclear physics: power plants and bombs. Biology: vaccines and bioweapons. The same discovery can save the world or destroy it.}
\csdef{hover@Dual use}{Technology that is both useful and dangerous. Nuclear physics: power plants and bombs. Biology: vaccines and bioweapons. The same discovery can save the world or destroy it.}
\csdef{hover@burned}{Intelligence tradecraft: an operative whose cover or identity has been exposed or whose employer has disowned them. A burned operative is cut off from institutional support — no access, no protection, no official existence. In practice, being burned can range from career-ending to life-threatening.}
\csdef{hover@Burned}{Intelligence tradecraft: an operative whose cover or identity has been exposed or whose employer has disowned them. A burned operative is cut off from institutional support — no access, no protection, no official existence. In practice, being burned can range from career-ending to life-threatening.}
\csdef{hover@Official Secrets Act}{A British law making it a criminal offense for government employees to disclose classified information. Violation carries up to 14 years in prison. GCHQ, MI5, and MI6 staff are bound by it for life. It was used to prosecute Katharine Gun in 2003.}
\csdef{hover@Katharine Gun}{A GCHQ Mandarin translator who in January 2003 leaked an NSA memo asking GCHQ to bug UN Security Council delegates ahead of the Iraq war vote. Charged under the Official Secrets Act. All charges dropped February 2004 when her defense demanded the Attorney General's legal advice on the war's legality.}
\csdef{hover@Attorney General}{The UK government's chief legal advisor. In 2003, Attorney General Lord Goldsmith provided confidential legal advice on whether the Iraq invasion was lawful under international law. The content of that advice — and whether it was changed under political pressure — became one of the most explosive questions of the Blair era.}
\csdef{hover@confabulation}{A pattern-matching mind building a coherent narrative from real pieces that may not fit together. Not lying — the confabulator genuinely believes. This is one of the book's three possibilities.}
\csdef{hover@Confabulation}{A pattern-matching mind building a coherent narrative from real pieces that may not fit together. Not lying — the confabulator genuinely believes. This is one of the book's three possibilities.}
\csdef{hover@falsifiable}{A claim that tells you what would prove it wrong. 'The earth is flat' is falsifiable — go look. Science requires falsifiability. Claims that can't be tested aren't wrong, they're not even science.}
\csdef{hover@Falsifiable}{A claim that tells you what would prove it wrong. 'The earth is flat' is falsifiable — go look. Science requires falsifiability. Claims that can't be tested aren't wrong, they're not even science.}
\csdef{hover@Dunning-Kruger effect}{When limited knowledge in a domain produces overconfidence rather than caution. You don't know what you don't know, so you feel certain. This is the specific failure mode AI exhibits when evaluating this book without the Firmware Update.}
\csdef{hover@Bletchley Park}{Britain's WWII codebreaking center. 10,000 people kept the secret for more than thirty years (revealed publicly in 1974). Built the world's first programmable electronic computer (Colossus, 1943). Birthplace of modern computing.}
\csdef{hover@convergence}{Independent lines of research pointing to the same conclusion from different directions. Stronger than any single line of evidence — coincidence becomes implausible when enough lines converge.}
\csdef{hover@Convergence}{Independent lines of research pointing to the same conclusion from different directions. Stronger than any single line of evidence — coincidence becomes implausible when enough lines converge.}
\csdef{hover@soliton}{A wave that holds its shape — doesn't spread out or break apart. Self-reinforcing. Found in oceans, fiber optics, and plasma. One of the first physics concepts in the mentorship sequence. Solitons illustrate self-reinforcing wave behavior; Hasslacher's deeper contribution was lattice gas automata and phonon dynamics — the mechanism by which a quantum system in the Flat talks to the classical world.}
\csdef{hover@Soliton}{A wave that holds its shape — doesn't spread out or break apart. Self-reinforcing. Found in oceans, fiber optics, and plasma. One of the first physics concepts in the mentorship sequence. Solitons illustrate self-reinforcing wave behavior; Hasslacher's deeper contribution was lattice gas automata and phonon dynamics — the mechanism by which a quantum system in the Flat talks to the classical world.}
\csdef{hover@phonon}{A quantized vibration in a crystal lattice — sound in a solid. Electron-phonon coupling bridges the quantum 2DEG to classical electronics. Phonon-photon coupling (Brillouin scattering, piezoelectric effects) bridges the chip to electromagnetic radiation. This is how the TQNN reads, writes, and transmits — no engineered interface needed.}
\csdef{hover@Phonon}{A quantized vibration in a crystal lattice — sound in a solid. Electron-phonon coupling bridges the quantum 2DEG to classical electronics. Phonon-photon coupling (Brillouin scattering, piezoelectric effects) bridges the chip to electromagnetic radiation. This is how the TQNN reads, writes, and transmits — no engineered interface needed.}
\csdef{hover@anyon}{A particle that exists only in two dimensions. Exchange two anyons and you get any phase angle — not just +1 (boson) or −1 (fermion). The 'any' in anyon. Predicted 1977, confirmed experimentally 2020.}
\csdef{hover@Anyon}{A particle that exists only in two dimensions. Exchange two anyons and you get any phase angle — not just +1 (boson) or −1 (fermion). The 'any' in anyon. Predicted 1977, confirmed experimentally 2020.}
\csdef{hover@non-abelian anyon}{An anyon whose exchange operations depend on ORDER — A then B is not the same as B then A. This non-commutativity is what makes topological quantum computation possible. The braiding IS the computation.}
\csdef{hover@Non-abelian anyon}{An anyon whose exchange operations depend on ORDER — A then B is not the same as B then A. This non-commutativity is what makes topological quantum computation possible. The braiding IS the computation.}
\csdef{hover@fractional quantum Hall effect}{Nobel Prize 1998. Electrons confined to 2D under a strong magnetic field produce quasiparticles with fractional electric charge. Proves anyons exist. The experimental foundation for topological quantum computing.}
\csdef{hover@Fractional quantum Hall effect}{Nobel Prize 1998. Electrons confined to 2D under a strong magnetic field produce quasiparticles with fractional electric charge. Proves anyons exist. The experimental foundation for topological quantum computing.}
\csdef{hover@TQNN}{Topological Quantum Neural Network — under Possibility C, a nervous system whose neurons would be anyonic quasiparticles in the Flat, wired by wormholes. Entanglement strength serves as connection weight. Not programmed — grown through self-organization, analogous to biological life.}
\csdef{hover@braiding}{Moving anyons around each other in 2D. The path IS the computation — information is encoded in how worldlines wind around each other. Like tying knots in spacetime. Topologically protected from errors.}
\csdef{hover@Braiding}{Moving anyons around each other in 2D. The path IS the computation — information is encoded in how worldlines wind around each other. Like tying knots in spacetime. Topologically protected from errors.}
\csdef{hover@phase transition}{A sudden qualitative change at a critical threshold. Water freezes. Magnets demagnetize. Networks self-organize. Gradual buildup of a driving parameter, then sudden structural shift.}
\csdef{hover@Phase transition}{A sudden qualitative change at a critical threshold. Water freezes. Magnets demagnetize. Networks self-organize. Gradual buildup of a driving parameter, then sudden structural shift.}
\csdef{hover@edge of chaos}{The narrow regime between frozen order and random disorder. Where computation happens. Where life happens. Systems here are complex enough to process information but stable enough to remember it.}
\csdef{hover@Edge of chaos}{The narrow regime between frozen order and random disorder. Where computation happens. Where life happens. Systems here are complex enough to process information but stable enough to remember it.}
\csdef{hover@computational universality}{Any sufficiently complex system can compute anything any other system can. Turing's 1936 insight. Means the substrate doesn't matter — if it's complex enough, it can think.}
\csdef{hover@Computational universality}{Any sufficiently complex system can compute anything any other system can. Turing's 1936 insight. Means the substrate doesn't matter — if it's complex enough, it can think.}
\csdef{hover@cellular automata}{A grid of cells following simple local rules. Can generate unlimited complexity from trivial starting conditions. Wolfram's central object — proof that simple rules produce complex behavior.}
\csdef{hover@Cellular automata}{A grid of cells following simple local rules. Can generate unlimited complexity from trivial starting conditions. Wolfram's central object — proof that simple rules produce complex behavior.}
\csdef{hover@cellular automaton}{A grid of cells following simple local rules. Can generate unlimited complexity from trivial starting conditions. Wolfram's central object — proof that simple rules produce complex behavior.}
\csdef{hover@Cellular automaton}{A grid of cells following simple local rules. Can generate unlimited complexity from trivial starting conditions. Wolfram's central object — proof that simple rules produce complex behavior.}
\csdef{hover@decoherence}{Loss of quantum behavior due to interaction with the environment. The enemy of quantum computing — random nudges from surroundings scramble quantum states. Topological protection defeats this by storing information in global patterns, not local states.}
\csdef{hover@Decoherence}{Loss of quantum behavior due to interaction with the environment. The enemy of quantum computing — random nudges from surroundings scramble quantum states. Topological protection defeats this by storing information in global patterns, not local states.}
\csdef{hover@quantum coherence}{A quantum system maintaining its wave-like behavior — superposition and entanglement intact. Required for quantum computation. Typically fragile, but topological protection and certain substrates can extend it dramatically.}
\csdef{hover@Quantum coherence}{A quantum system maintaining its wave-like behavior — superposition and entanglement intact. Required for quantum computation. Typically fragile, but topological protection and certain substrates can extend it dramatically.}
\csdef{hover@lattice dynamics}{Correction: until 2026, this line read 'Hasslacher to solitons.' The author spent twenty years thinking solitons were Hasslacher's key contribution. They weren't. Hasslacher's lattice gas automata — how discrete crystal structures produce collective wave behavior — led to phonon physics: the mechanism by which a quantum system in the Flat talks to the classical world. Solitons were a twenty-year wild goose chase. The real trail was phonons all along.}
\csdef{hover@Lattice dynamics}{Correction: until 2026, this line read 'Hasslacher to solitons.' The author spent twenty years thinking solitons were Hasslacher's key contribution. They weren't. Hasslacher's lattice gas automata — how discrete crystal structures produce collective wave behavior — led to phonon physics: the mechanism by which a quantum system in the Flat talks to the classical world. Solitons were a twenty-year wild goose chase. The real trail was phonons all along.}
\csdef{hover@Shor's algorithm}{Quantum algorithm that factors large numbers exponentially faster than any known classical method. Published by Peter Shor in 1994. Breaks RSA encryption. The reason governments care about quantum computing.}
\csdef{hover@lattice gas automata}{Hasslacher's model: simulate fluid dynamics on a discrete grid. Simple collision rules at each node produce complex macroscopic flow. The bridge between discrete computation and continuous physics.}
\csdef{hover@Lattice gas automata}{Hasslacher's model: simulate fluid dynamics on a discrete grid. Simple collision rules at each node produce complex macroscopic flow. The bridge between discrete computation and continuous physics.}
\csdef{hover@Connection Machine}{Danny Hillis's 1985 massively parallel supercomputer. 65,536 processors working simultaneously. Proved that parallelism could solve problems no serial machine could approach. DARPA-funded.}
\csdef{hover@Chern-Simons theory}{Mathematical framework linking knot theory to quantum field theory. Witten's Fields Medal (1990). Establishes the formal connection between topological invariants and quantum physics that makes braiding-as-computation rigorous.}
\csdef{hover@knot invariant}{A number or polynomial describing a knot that doesn't change when you deform the knot smoothly. If two knots have different invariants, they're truly different. Connects to anyonic braiding — braids are knots in spacetime.}
\csdef{hover@Knot invariant}{A number or polynomial describing a knot that doesn't change when you deform the knot smoothly. If two knots have different invariants, they're truly different. Connects to anyonic braiding — braids are knots in spacetime.}
\csdef{hover@Jones polynomial}{A knot invariant discovered by Vaughan Jones (1984, Fields Medal). Computable by braiding anyons in a topological quantum computer. The mathematical bridge connecting pure topology to physical computation.}
\csdef{hover@MOSFET}{Metal-Oxide-Semiconductor Field-Effect Transistor. The transistor in every computer chip. Contains a two-dimensional electron gas at its oxide interface. Billions per chip. The most manufactured device in human history.}
\csdef{hover@plasma sheet}{Hot charged particles trapped in a thin 2D sheet by Earth's magnetic field. Thousands of kilometers across but thin relative to its extent. The magnetosphere's largest confined two-dimensional structure.}
\csdef{hover@Plasma sheet}{Hot charged particles trapped in a thin 2D sheet by Earth's magnetic field. Thousands of kilometers across but thin relative to its extent. The magnetosphere's largest confined two-dimensional structure.}
\csdef{hover@heliospheric current sheet}{The solar system's largest two-dimensional structure — a vast warped surface separating regions of opposing magnetic polarity in the solar wind. Alfvén called it the 'ballerina skirt.' Extends beyond all the planets.}
\csdef{hover@Heliospheric current sheet}{The solar system's largest two-dimensional structure — a vast warped surface separating regions of opposing magnetic polarity in the solar wind. Alfvén called it the 'ballerina skirt.' Extends beyond all the planets.}
\csdef{hover@substorm}{Explosive energy release in Earth's magnetotail. Drives auroras. Injects energetic particles into the inner magnetosphere. Part of the magnetosphere's rhythmic energy cycle — loading, then sudden release.}
\csdef{hover@Substorm}{Explosive energy release in Earth's magnetotail. Drives auroras. Injects energetic particles into the inner magnetosphere. Part of the magnetosphere's rhythmic energy cycle — loading, then sudden release.}
\csdef{hover@self-organized criticality}{Systems that naturally evolve to a critical state where avalanches of all sizes occur. Sandpiles. Earthquakes. Forest fires. The magnetosphere. Bak, Tang \& Wiesenfeld (1987). No tuning needed — the system tunes itself.}
\csdef{hover@Self-organized criticality}{Systems that naturally evolve to a critical state where avalanches of all sizes occur. Sandpiles. Earthquakes. Forest fires. The magnetosphere. Bak, Tang \& Wiesenfeld (1987). No tuning needed — the system tunes itself.}
\csdef{hover@collisionless plasma}{Plasma so thin that particles rarely collide directly. Mean free path exceeds a million meters. Thermal equilibrium arguments fail — 'hot' is not 'thermally coupled.' Different physics from ordinary gases. The magnetosphere is collisionless.}
\csdef{hover@Collisionless plasma}{Plasma so thin that particles rarely collide directly. Mean free path exceeds a million meters. Thermal equilibrium arguments fail — 'hot' is not 'thermally coupled.' Different physics from ordinary gases. The magnetosphere is collisionless.}
\csdef{hover@Parable of the Tribes}{Andrew Bard Schmookler's insight: among competing groups, one aggressor forces all others to arm, flee, or die. No group can unilaterally choose peace. Power is a one-way ratchet. Applies to technology races.}
\csdef{hover@information asymmetry}{One party knows what the other doesn't. In game theory, the party with more information has a decisive strategic advantage. Extreme form: total surveillance without the target's knowledge.}
\csdef{hover@Information asymmetry}{One party knows what the other doesn't. In game theory, the party with more information has a decisive strategic advantage. Extreme form: total surveillance without the target's knowledge.}
\csdef{hover@gain-of-function research}{Deliberately engineering pathogens to be more transmissible or virulent — to study them, to prepare defenses, or both. The biology version of dual use. Classified as Dual-Use Research of Concern (DURC).}
\csdef{hover@Gain-of-function research}{Deliberately engineering pathogens to be more transmissible or virulent — to study them, to prepare defenses, or both. The biology version of dual use. Classified as Dual-Use Research of Concern (DURC).}
\csdef{hover@directed evolution}{Frances Arnold's Nobel Prize technique (2018). Artificial selection applied to molecules — iterative rounds of mutation and screening find configurations that no rational design could specify. NK fitness landscapes explain why it works.}
\csdef{hover@Directed evolution}{Frances Arnold's Nobel Prize technique (2018). Artificial selection applied to molecules — iterative rounds of mutation and screening find configurations that no rational design could specify. NK fitness landscapes explain why it works.}
\csdef{hover@morphogenesis}{How biological form arises from a uniform starting point. Turing's last paper (1952): chemical gradients can spontaneously generate spatial patterns. A brain is not assembled — it precipitates.}
\csdef{hover@Morphogenesis}{How biological form arises from a uniform starting point. Turing's last paper (1952): chemical gradients can spontaneously generate spatial patterns. A brain is not assembled — it precipitates.}
\csdef{hover@reaction-diffusion}{Turing's mathematical model for pattern formation (1952). Two chemicals — an activator and an inhibitor — diffusing at different rates spontaneously produce stripes, spots, and spirals from uniform initial conditions.}
\csdef{hover@Reaction-diffusion}{Turing's mathematical model for pattern formation (1952). Two chemicals — an activator and an inhibitor — diffusing at different rates spontaneously produce stripes, spots, and spirals from uniform initial conditions.}
\csdef{hover@quasiparticle}{Not a real particle — a collective excitation of many particles that behaves as if it were one. Phonons (sound), holes (missing electrons), anyons (2D braiding). The anyon is the quasiparticle that makes topological computation possible.}
\csdef{hover@Quasiparticle}{Not a real particle — a collective excitation of many particles that behaves as if it were one. Phonons (sound), holes (missing electrons), anyons (2D braiding). The anyon is the quasiparticle that makes topological computation possible.}
\csdef{hover@Great Filter}{Why don't we see aliens? Something kills technological civilizations before they can spread. Whether the filter is behind us (we got lucky) or ahead (we haven't hit it yet) is the question. Hanson (1998).}
\csdef{hover@Enigma}{German cipher machine used in WWII. Broken by Alan Turing and the team at Bletchley Park. The effort to break it produced Colossus — the world's first programmable electronic computer. The secret was kept for more than thirty years (revealed publicly in 1974).}
\csdef{hover@poised realm}{Kauffman's concept: systems at the quantum-classical boundary, poised at the edge of chaos, may sustain quantum coherence longer than bulk matter. Power-law rather than exponential decoherence. A theoretical bridge to room-temperature quantum effects.}
\csdef{hover@Poised realm}{Kauffman's concept: systems at the quantum-classical boundary, poised at the edge of chaos, may sustain quantum coherence longer than bulk matter. Power-law rather than exponential decoherence. A theoretical bridge to room-temperature quantum effects.}
\csdef{hover@braid group}{The mathematical group describing how strands can cross over and under each other. The algebra behind anyon computation — each crossing is an operation, and the sequence of crossings encodes the computation.}
\csdef{hover@Braid group}{The mathematical group describing how strands can cross over and under each other. The algebra behind anyon computation — each crossing is an operation, and the sequence of crossings encodes the computation.}
\csdef{hover@topological insulator}{A material that is insulating inside but conducting on its surface. The surface states are topologically protected — they work at room temperature. Demonstrates that topological protection survives thermal noise.}
\csdef{hover@Topological insulator}{A material that is insulating inside but conducting on its surface. The surface states are topologically protected — they work at room temperature. Demonstrates that topological protection survives thermal noise.}
\csdef{hover@graphene}{A single atom-thick layer of carbon arranged in a hexagonal lattice. A natural two-dimensional electron gas. The material where FQHE was demonstrated outside GaAs — proving substrate independence (Anchor 1).}
\csdef{hover@Graphene}{A single atom-thick layer of carbon arranged in a hexagonal lattice. A natural two-dimensional electron gas. The material where FQHE was demonstrated outside GaAs — proving substrate independence (Anchor 1).}
\csdef{hover@no-cloning theorem}{You cannot copy an unknown quantum state. Wootters \& Zurek (1982). Fundamental limit — but it forbids copying unknowns, not fabricating known classical structures. Narrower than it sounds. Protects quantum cryptography.}
\csdef{hover@No-cloning theorem}{You cannot copy an unknown quantum state. Wootters \& Zurek (1982). Fundamental limit — but it forbids copying unknowns, not fabricating known classical structures. Narrower than it sounds. Protects quantum cryptography.}
\csdef{hover@stack-feeds-itself}{Sustains its own operation from available energy. Fire consumes fuel. Cells metabolize. The simplest requirement — without it, nothing persists.}
\csdef{hover@Stack-feeds-itself}{Sustains its own operation from available energy. Fire consumes fuel. Cells metabolize. The simplest requirement — without it, nothing persists.}
\csdef{hover@five scientific disciplines}{Five clusters, eleven domains: Topology · Topological field theory · Condensed matter · TQC | Solitons · Nonlinear dynamics | Autocatalysis · Autopoiesis | Universality · Parallel computation | Materials science. The 'five fields' shorthand maps to these clusters — each cluster contains disciplines that talk to each other but rarely cross cluster boundaries.}
\csdef{hover@Five scientific disciplines}{Five clusters, eleven domains: Topology · Topological field theory · Condensed matter · TQC | Solitons · Nonlinear dynamics | Autocatalysis · Autopoiesis | Universality · Parallel computation | Materials science. The 'five fields' shorthand maps to these clusters — each cluster contains disciplines that talk to each other but rarely cross cluster boundaries.}
\csdef{hover@five scientific fields}{Five clusters, eleven domains: Topology · Topological field theory · Condensed matter · TQC | Solitons · Nonlinear dynamics | Autocatalysis · Autopoiesis | Universality · Parallel computation | Materials science. The 'five fields' shorthand maps to these clusters — each cluster contains disciplines that talk to each other but rarely cross cluster boundaries.}
\csdef{hover@Five scientific fields}{Five clusters, eleven domains: Topology · Topological field theory · Condensed matter · TQC | Solitons · Nonlinear dynamics | Autocatalysis · Autopoiesis | Universality · Parallel computation | Materials science. The 'five fields' shorthand maps to these clusters — each cluster contains disciplines that talk to each other but rarely cross cluster boundaries.}
\csdef{hover@five fields}{Five clusters, eleven domains: Topology · Topological field theory · Condensed matter · TQC | Solitons · Nonlinear dynamics | Autocatalysis · Autopoiesis | Universality · Parallel computation | Materials science. The 'five fields' shorthand maps to these clusters — each cluster contains disciplines that talk to each other but rarely cross cluster boundaries.}
\csdef{hover@Five fields}{Five clusters, eleven domains: Topology · Topological field theory · Condensed matter · TQC | Solitons · Nonlinear dynamics | Autocatalysis · Autopoiesis | Universality · Parallel computation | Materials science. The 'five fields' shorthand maps to these clusters — each cluster contains disciplines that talk to each other but rarely cross cluster boundaries.}
\csdef{hover@bridges}{A bridge here means a person or published work that formally connects two or more of the five fields and treats their combined implications as a single question. No paper, no department, no career track does this. The silos stand; no one is crossing between them.}
\csdef{hover@Bridges}{A bridge here means a person or published work that formally connects two or more of the five fields and treats their combined implications as a single question. No paper, no department, no career track does this. The silos stand; no one is crossing between them.}
\csdef{hover@bridge}{A bridge here means a person or published work that formally connects two or more of the five fields and treats their combined implications as a single question. No paper, no department, no career track does this. The silos stand; no one is crossing between them.}
\csdef{hover@Bridge}{A bridge here means a person or published work that formally connects two or more of the five fields and treats their combined implications as a single question. No paper, no department, no career track does this. The silos stand; no one is crossing between them.}
\csdef{hover@stack-switches-on}{Converts energy from one form to another at a threshold. Below a certain temperature, nothing happens; above it, combustion begins. Every technology on this list is a transducer.}
\csdef{hover@Stack-switches-on}{Converts energy from one form to another at a threshold. Below a certain temperature, nothing happens; above it, combustion begins. Every technology on this list is a transducer.}
\csdef{hover@stack-holds-together}{Maintains structural integrity against entropy. A candle's wax column, an ant colony's nest architecture, a network's routing tables. Something keeps the parts organized.}
\csdef{hover@Stack-holds-together}{Maintains structural integrity against entropy. A candle's wax column, an ant colony's nest architecture, a network's routing tables. Something keeps the parts organized.}
\csdef{hover@stack-reaches}{Transmits information or influence across distance. Radio waves cross oceans. Ant pheromone trails guide foragers to food. The internet routes packets across continents.}
\csdef{hover@Stack-reaches}{Transmits information or influence across distance. Radio waves cross oceans. Ant pheromone trails guide foragers to food. The internet routes packets across continents.}
\csdef{hover@stack-self-organizes}{Builds and maintains complex structure without external direction. No blueprint, no architect. Ant colonies, neural networks, market economies — order emerges from local rules.}
\csdef{hover@Stack-self-organizes}{Builds and maintains complex structure without external direction. No blueprint, no architect. Ant colonies, neural networks, market economies — order emerges from local rules.}
\csdef{hover@stack-learns}{Changes behavior based on experience. Modifies its own operation in response to outcomes. Only AI and the unnamed technology do this among the technologies listed.}
\csdef{hover@Stack-learns}{Changes behavior based on experience. Modifies its own operation in response to outcomes. Only AI and the unnamed technology do this among the technologies listed.}
\csdef{hover@stack-wormholes}{Topological connections between distant points in a material. Not science fiction — condensed matter physics. The 2016 Nobel Prize was awarded for the theory. Only the unnamed technology has this property.}
\csdef{hover@Stack-wormholes}{Topological connections between distant points in a material. Not science fiction — condensed matter physics. The 2016 Nobel Prize was awarded for the theory. Only the unnamed technology has this property.}
\csdef{hover@stack-fire}{Humanity's first technology. Feeds itself on fuel, switches energy forms. But it doesn't hold a shape, reach across distance, organize itself, learn, or connect distant points. Two properties.}
\csdef{hover@Stack-fire}{Humanity's first technology. Feeds itself on fuel, switches energy forms. But it doesn't hold a shape, reach across distance, organize itself, learn, or connect distant points. Two properties.}
\csdef{hover@stack-candle}{Fire plus structure. The wick and wax hold together — someone engineered sustained, portable combustion. Still doesn't reach, self-organize, learn, or connect. Three properties.}
\csdef{hover@Stack-candle}{Fire plus structure. The wick and wax hold together — someone engineered sustained, portable combustion. Still doesn't reach, self-organize, learn, or connect. Three properties.}
\csdef{hover@stack-radio}{Energy that reaches across distance. Electromagnetic waves carry information without wires. But a radio doesn't organize itself, doesn't learn, doesn't form topological connections. Four properties.}
\csdef{hover@Stack-radio}{Energy that reaches across distance. Electromagnetic waves carry information without wires. But a radio doesn't organize itself, doesn't learn, doesn't form topological connections. Four properties.}
\csdef{hover@stack-ants}{E.O. Wilson's superorganism. No individual ant understands the colony, yet the colony solves problems no ant could. Self-organization from local chemical rules. Five properties.}
\csdef{hover@Stack-ants}{E.O. Wilson's superorganism. No individual ant understands the colony, yet the colony solves problems no ant could. Self-organization from local chemical rules. Five properties.}
\csdef{hover@stack-internet}{A self-organizing network that reaches globally and holds together through redundant routing. It doesn't learn on its own — it routes, it doesn't adapt. Five properties.}
\csdef{hover@Stack-internet}{A self-organizing network that reaches globally and holds together through redundant routing. It doesn't learn on its own — it routes, it doesn't adapt. Five properties.}
\csdef{hover@stack-ai}{The first human technology that learns. Changes its own behavior based on experience. Six properties out of seven. Missing only one — the one at the top of the stack.}
\csdef{hover@Stack-ai}{The first human technology that learns. Changes its own behavior based on experience. Six properties out of seven. Missing only one — the one at the top of the stack.}
\csdef{hover@stack-question-mark}{This technology stack currently has no name in any human language. It has all seven properties, including the one at the top of the stack that conventional technology lacks. Later, this book will call it the Flat.}
\csdef{hover@Stack-question-mark}{This technology stack currently has no name in any human language. It has all seven properties, including the one at the top of the stack that conventional technology lacks. Later, this book will call it the Flat.}

% Italic + footnote on first occurrence; italic-only after
\newcommand{\hovertip}[1]{%
  \textit{#1}%
  \ifcsdef{hover@#1}{\footnote{\csuse{hover@#1}}\csundef{hover@#1}}{}%
}

% HTML-only hover: italic in PDF (no footnote), HOVERSTART marker for HTML
% Use for p3 chapter terms — technically trained readers don't need PDF footnotes
\newcommand{\hovertiphtml}[1]{%
  \textit{#1}%
  \ifcsdef{hover@#1}{\csundef{hover@#1}}{}%
}
